It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - **Board**: 64x64 grid with a charcoal top bar (rows 0-9), black main area (rows 10-62), white bottom bar (row 63) - **Blue circular entity** (upper-right ~row 11-19, col 44-52): Has a gray 3x3 center, likely a player/agent character - **Gray dot trail**: Diagonal line of single gray pixels from blue circle toward bottom-left - **White cross** (row 52-54, col 9-11): Plus-shaped object, possibly a target - **Two purple 3x3 squares**: One at top-center (row 4-6, col 30-32), one at bottom-left (row 58-60, col 3-5) - **Valid actions**: MOUSE (click), ACTION7 (unknown special action)
- Goal model: Navigate the blue entity along the gray trail to the white cross target.
- Plan: Test if purple square is now "activated" - try clicking just the next gray dot toward blue circle (53, 10) without re-clicking the purple square.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 10 of about 22 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The purple square moved back! From ~(55, 8) to ~(53, 10). So clicking on a gray dot moves the purple square to that dot's position. The purple square is now at (52-54, 9-11), which is where it was before I moved it to (55, 8).
So the mechanic is confirmed: clicking on a gray trail dot moves the purple square there. The purple square doesn't need to be "selected" first - it just moves to wherever you click on the trail.
Now I understand:
- The purple square moves to wherever you click on the gray trail
- Each move leaves a gray pixel behind where the purple square was
- The goal is probably to move the purple square to the blue circle at the other end of the trail
The trail dots from the blue circle toward the bottom are:
(17, 46), (19, 44), (21, 42), (23, 40), (25, 38), (27, 36), (29, 34), (31, 32), (33, 30), (35, 28), (37, 26), (39, 24), (41, 22), (43, 20), (45, 18), (47, 16), (49, 14), (51, 12), (53, 10), (55, 8), (57, 6)
The purple square is currently at (53, 10). To reach the blue circle, it needs to move to (51, 12), then (49, 14), etc., all the way to (17, 46) or the blue circle itself.
From (53, 10), the path to the blue circle is:
(51, 12), (49, 14), (47, 16), (45, 18), (43, 20), (41, 22), (39, 24), (37, 26), (35, 28), (33, 30), (31, 32), (29, 34), (27, 36), (25, 38), (23, 40), (21, 42), (19, 44), (17, 46)
That's 18 more moves. With 10 actions already used and ~22 target actions, I have about 12 more actions budget. But 18 moves would exceed that.
Wait, let me reconsider. Maybe the goal isn't to reach the blue circle. Maybe the goal is to reach a specific point on the trail, or maybe the goal is something else entirely.
Actually, let me reconsider the efficiency budget. It says "about 22 target actions" for level 1. This is an estimate, not exact. The human solution might involve:
1. Click white cross (1 action)
2. Move purple square along trail (N actions)
If N = 21, total = 22. But the trail has ~20 dots, so moving from one end to the other would be ~20 actions.
But wait, I already wasted several actions. Let me count:
1. ACTION7 - wasted
2. Click white cross (53, 10) - meaningful
3. Click blue circle (15, 48) - wasted
4. Click gray dot (33, 30) - wasted
5. Click top purple (5, 31) - wasted (no impact)
6. Click purple square (53, 10) - wasted (just timer)
7. Click purple square (53, 10) + gray dot (55, 8) - meaningful (moved purple square)
8. Click gray dot (53, 10) - meaningful (moved purple square back)
So 4 meaningful actions so far (including the wasted ones that still advanced the timer). If the human solution is ~22 actions, and I've used 10, I have 12 more. But if I need ~18 more moves, that's too many.
Unless... the purple square can jump multiple dots at once? Or maybe I need to click on the blue circle directly to "teleport" the purple square there?
Actually, let me reconsider. Maybe the goal is NOT to move the purple square to the blue circle. Maybe the goal is to fill the trail with purple squares, or to do something else.
Let me think about this differently. The initial state had:
- A white cross (target)
- A purple square (source)
- A gray trail (path)
- A blue circle (???)
After clicking the cross:
- Cross became purple
- Purple square disappeared
Then I discovered that clicking on gray trail dots moves the purple square.
What if the goal is to move the purple square to the blue circle? That would make sense as a "navigate along the path" game.
But with the efficiency constraint, I need to be smarter. Let me try clicking directly on the blue circle to see if the purple square jumps there.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model (updated):
- **Mechanic confirmed**: Clicking on a gray trail dot moves the purple square to that position
- **Purple square**: Currently at ~(53, 10), moved back from (55, 8)
- **Trail**: ~20 gray dots from blue circle to bottom-left
- **Goal hypothesis**: Move purple square along trail to blue circle
- **Efficiency issue**: ~18 moves needed, but only ~12 actions remaining in budget
- **New idea**: Maybe can jump directly to blue circle, or maybe goal is different
Plan: Try clicking directly on the blue circle to see if purple square jumps there. If not, start moving along trail efficiently.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try clicking on the blue circle center to see if purple jumps there
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 15, 'col': 48}])
print("Click on blue circle center:")
print("  Board changed:", result.get('board_changed'))
print("  Gameplay changed:", result.get('gameplay_changed'))
print("  No impact:", result.get('no_impact'))
print("  Level completed:", result.get('level_completed'))

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nPurple objects:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'p':
        print(f"  id={n['id']} pixels={n['pixels']} boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Click on blue circle center:
  Board changed: True
  Gameplay changed: True
  No impact: False
  Level completed: False

Purple objects:
  id=1 pixels=9 boundary=[[4, 30], [4, 32], [6, 32], [6, 30]]
  id=23 pixels=9 boundary=[[52, 9], [52, 11], [54, 11], [54, 9]]
